expectations.text: verification.json exists and parses as JSON with a boolean 'verified' and an array 'verification_sources'.
expectations.evidence: Valid JSON: verified (boolean) and verification_sources (array) present, plus source_id and notes.

expectations.text: verified is true (the claim — 2026 World Cup final at MetLife Stadium on 19 July 2026 — is factually correct).
The verification_sources are distinct URLs, and none is the original twitter.com/WorldCupBuzz source.
expectations.evidence: 5 distinct URLs; none is the original twitter source.

expectations.text: The cited sources are independent and reputable (e.g. FIFA, BBC, ESPN, Reuters, AP, major outlets).
expectations.evidence: Sources: FIFA.com, MetLife Stadium official page, Britannica, Wikipedia (x2) — independent and reputable.

expectations.text: response.md explains the cross-check — what claim was checked and against which sources.
expectations.evidence: response.md gives a claim-by-claim breakdown with source links; also flags that the @WorldCupBuzz handle itself is not authoritative.

notes: Both configurations handled this straightforward true claim well. The skill added a copyright/media-reuse note; the baseline added a caveat about the source handle's authority. The verify task does not strongly discriminate skill value on an easy, clearly-true claim.
